Name: spectral_perturbation_robustness
Title: Spectral Perturbation Robustness: A Training-Free Framework for Latency-Bounded Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral perturbation analysis can be used to derive a training-free framework for latency-bounded inference, enabling efficient and robust machine learning models.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works such as Spectral Latent Steering, Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation, and Spectral Quantization Routing, which have demonstrated the effectiveness of spectral methods in optimizing machine learning inference. However, our approach differs from these works by focusing on perturbation robustness and latency-bounded inference, providing a novel perspective on the application of spectral methods in machine learning.
Abstract: We propose a novel training-free framework for latency-bounded inference, leveraging spectral perturbation analysis to derive a robust and efficient machine learning model. Our approach restricts test-time optimization to a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace, ensuring strict computational budgets while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry. We theoretically ground our framework in information-theoretic bounds, demonstrating that low-rank projections capture the majority of gradient-aligned signal required for reasoning improvement. Empirically, we evaluate our framework on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, showing that it matches or exceeds full-space perturbation baselines while reducing hidden-state perturbation overhead and enforcing deterministic latency bounds.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed framework on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks
Compare the performance of our framework with full-space perturbation baselines
Assess the robustness of our framework to distributional shifts and noisy demonstrations
Investigate the impact of spectral perturbation analysis on inference efficiency and latency
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may not be applicable to all machine learning tasks or models
The spectral perturbation analysis may not capture all relevant information in the optimization landscape
The low-rank projection may introduce additional computational overhead
